---
bibliography: refs.bib
---

## Results Discussion
The results suggest that there are significantly more sightings (@fig-dis_spot B) and a higher dolphin density (@fig-kde_map) at the southern tip of Gibraltar (Europa Point). This may be explained by the geographical characteristics of the area, as the currents around this point are the strongest among the three sampling points, leading to increased upwelling and the creation of a favourable foraging ground for cetaceans [@sanchez-garridoAlboranSeaCirculation2022]. Moreover, the southern area, although still experiencing high levels of traffic, exhibits a lower frequency of ship bunkering compared to other areas. This lower traffic could benefit cetaceans and also increase the detectability from the vantage points. Conversely, this observation could be explained by the elevation of the observation point, which was the lowest of all points surveyed (@tbl-landspots). Consequently, the probability of detecting dolphins in proximity to the shoreline is increased in comparison to vantage points situated at higher altitudes above sea level.


<!-- Comment on Olayas study addressing the southern location of PG -->
Conversely, previous studies by Olaya-Ponzone et al. [-@olaya-ponzoneSpatioTemporalPatternsLimited2026; -@olaya-ponzoneMixedspeciesDolphinGroups2025] suggested that sightings on the west side were expected to be the highest. These studies indicated that the detection of dolphins was highest inside the bay and around the deep underwater canyon inside the Bay of Gibraltar. Contrary to this, the number of sightings on the west side was comparatively low when assessed per unit effort. It has been hypothesized that the frequent use of the Bay of Gibraltar by commercial ships for bunkering and as a main anchorage in a heavily trafficked area may be the reason for the restricted view while surveying. This may result in the potential limitations to observe groups. Moreover, the elevated perspective affording a view of the bay is situated in a relatively southerly direction. This geographical positioning presents a challenge in effectively surveying the northern section of the bay, particularly with regard to accurately confirming species and quantifying group size.

<!-- Change Stats after finishing analysis -->
### Statistical Analysis
Encounter rates, expressed as sightings per unit effort (SPUE), varied significantly among observation stations **(Kruskal–Wallis: χ² = 11.61, df = 2, p = 0.003)**, being highest at Europa, which differed significantly from Prince George after correction for multiple comparisons **(Dunn's test, Benjamini–Hochberg-adjusted: p = 0.002)**. This indicates spatial heterogeneity in cetacean encounter rate across the surveyed sectors rather than a uniform distribution within the BGTW. Importantly, however, SPUE is an effort-standardised index of relative encounter rate and is not corrected for imperfect or distance-dependent detection; because the stations differed in observer height, angular sector width and the extent of water overlooked, part of this variation may reflect between-station differences in detectability and surveyed area rather than true differences in animal density [@bucklandIntroductionDistanceSampling2001]. The significant contrast should therefore be read as a difference in observed encounter rate, with confirmation of a genuine density gradient requiring detection-corrected estimates. Notwithstanding this caveat, the consistently elevated SPUE at Europa identifies it as a candidate area of elevated cetacean occurrence and a priority for targeted monitoring.

<!-- Change Stats after finishing analysis -->
### Detection Function
Reliable estimation of a detection function typically requires a minimum of 75-100 detections, with larger samples needed to fit covariates or more complex key functions[@bucklandIntroductionDistanceSampling2001].In the present study, 121 on-effort cetacean sightings were obtained in total; however, because detection functions were fitted separately for each station, the relevant sample size is the per-station count. Even the best-sampled station (Europa) yielded only 54 detections, with the remaining stations sampled considerably less, so all per-station detection functions were fitted below the recommended minimum. This shortfall—driven by the constrained field season and the mid-study change from a boat-based line-transect to a land-based station design—means the fitted detection functions, and the density estimates derived from them, are imprecise and should be interpreted with caution.

Another caveat of this approach was already expressed in previous studies [@keenDeterminingMarineMammal2021; @thomasDistanceSoftwareDesign2010; @howeDistanceSamplingCamera2017] concerns the assumptions of a fixed observation station. Because each station repeatedly scanned the same fixed sector rather than sampling randomly placed transects, animals cannot be assumed to be distributed independently of distance from the observer; moreover, detectability—modelled as a function of distance alone—may in practice have varied across the sector with bearing-dependent conditions such as glare and sea state. Neither assumption could be verified within the constrained timeframe, and both warrant explicit attention in future station-based surveys.



### Kernel Density Estimation
As implemented here, `#gls("kde")`{=typst} yields an aggregate, area-wide description of relative cetacean density. Because all on-effort sightings are pooled and weighted only by group size, the resulting surface reflects the combined space use of every detected animal and cannot resolve heterogeneity in habitat use among social groups or individuals. Distinct groups that occupy the same broad area while maintaining non-overlapping core areas are therefore indistinguishable within the pooled utilisation surface, and any fine-scale spatial partitioning is averaged out [@fiebergQuantifyingHomeRangeOverlap2005]. In the absence of individual- or group-level identification, for example through photo-identification or biotelemetry, the KDE surface characterises overall occurrence and areas of elevated density rather than group- or individual-level habitat utilisation.
Furthermore non-parametric estimators that construct the utilisation distribution from local geometry, such as the `#gls("locoh")`{=typst} method [@getzLoCoHNonparametericKernel2007], could refine the spatial representation of cetacean occurrence. Unlike the fixed-kernel approach applied here, `#gls("locoh")`{=typst} builds the distribution from local convex hulls of neighbouring sightings and therefore respects hard boundaries and interior exclusions—such as the coastline and the landmasses within the study area—rather than smoothing density across areas the animals cannot occupy.


## Data Collection
As outlined in the [Methods](#methods-sec), the survey design had to be revised from vessel-based line transects covering the entire `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst} to land-based observations covering only part of the study area. This change was necessitated by logistical constraints beyond the researcher's control: the intended survey vessel could not be launched, as arrangements for its transport and craning into the marina were not completed within the field-season window. Despite repeated assurances that the vessel would be made available, it did not enter the water, and, in consultation with the project supervisor, continued waiting was judged unfeasible given the fixed timeframe. Because the survey and sampling schedule had already been planned and proposed around vessel-based sampling, this represented one of the most significant project risks to be realised. The survey was consequently redesigned to accommodate these constraints.
As a result, the land-based stations sampled only parts of the `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst}, and the density and distribution estimates should therefore be regarded as representative of the surveyed sectors rather than of the territory as a whole.

Due to the complexity of the Gibraltar coastline as well as the funnel effect from the Strait of Gibraltar the sea state could vary within one sector. Even though the sea state was assessed before every watch this resulted in possibly missed observation even though the sea state overall was suitable [@giacomaAreLandBased2013; @keenDeterminingMarineMammal2020]. Glare and swell height are likewise expected to lower detection probability; although both were recorded, they were not incorporated as covariates in the **detection function**, for example through multiple-covariate distance sampling [@thomasDistanceSoftwareDesign2010; @marquesDistanceSamplingEstimating2009], nor reflected in the effort-uncorrected kernel density surface.
<!-- Check with Stephen if I should add this -->

As described in the Methods, reticle binoculars were used to measure the vertical angle between each sighting and the horizon, which—together with the observer's height—was used to derive the radial distance to the sighting (see [Calculations](#calc-dis)). Although the reticle method is a well-established and reliable approach for shore-based distance estimation [@lerczakCalculatingSightingDistances1998], it discretises distance, because reticle graticules resolve angles only in fixed increments. To reduce this coarseness, observers were instructed to interpolate to the nearest quarter-reticle rather than reading only the half-reticle marks. The effect is nonetheless most pronounced at long range: because distance is a non-linear (approximately reciprocal) function of the depression angle, a single reticle interval subtends a progressively larger distance band as range increases, such that one reticle may span several kilometres at the far edge of the sampled area. This limitation is highlighted at low observer heights and was therefore most relevant at Europa (18 m a.s.l.). Although in practice most sightings there were recorded relatively close to shore, limiting its influence on the results.

Furthermore land-based distance sampling has rarely been adapted to small cetaceans, as their smaller size and short surfacing times with little to no indicators, makes them harder to spot from a distance compared to larger cetaceans. When applied on small cetaceans [@giacomaAreLandBased2013; @emblingHowMuchEffort2015; @keenDeterminingMarineMammal2020] the surveys were typically supplemented with a camera or even an infrared camera to improve detection.  Successful land-based studies of small cetaceans nonetheless exist, although they have predominantly targeted larger-bodied delphinidae, such as *Tursiops truncatus* [@gutierrez-munozPatternsTrendsCetacean2021].



# SWOT Analysis

```{=typst}
#[
    #show table.cell.where(y: 0): set text(size: 16pt, weight: "bold")
    #table(
    columns: (1fr, 1fr),
    stroke: none,
    align: (x, y) => if y == 0 { center } else { left + top },
    table.vline(x: 1, stroke: 0.5pt),
    table.header(
        table.cell(inset: (bottom: 25pt))[#smallcaps()[Strengths]],
        [#smallcaps[Weaknesses]],
    ),
    [
        - High sensitivity
        - Low reagent cost
    ],
    [
        - Limited throughput
        - Requires frequent calibration
    ],
    )
]



```
## Strength
- Costs
- Low effort
- Easy to apply and teach

## Weaknesses
- Time intensive
- Weather dependent
- Relies on detectability of Cetaceans (easier for larger cetaceans or larger groups)

## Opportunities

## Threats

# Conclusion
The author highly encourages to conduct follow-up studies and continue systematically assessing the distribution of cetaceans in the BGTW. As this is the only method that avoids biases introduced by only sampling parts of the whole area and gives us a closer picture of reality.
